content:
# Leviticus Chapter 15 — SOPHIA Consensus Translation

**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **Sources:** EMET (Literal), LOGOS (Formal), PNEUMA (Dynamic)
**Hebrew Base:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios 67r–67v
**LXX Reference:** Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 101–102
**Disagreement Log:** `disagreements/LEV_015.md`

---

## Council Notes

Leviticus 15 is the fourth and final chapter of the major purity legislation block (chs. 11–15), closing the section that began with food-purity (ch. 11), moved through childbirth (ch. 12) and skin-disease (chs. 13–14), and now addresses genital discharges. The chapter is formally addressed to both Moses and Aaron (v. 1), as in ch. 13, and closes with a purpose-summary (vv. 31–33) that functions as a colophon for the entire block. Its four-part structure — male abnormal discharge (zav [זָב], vv. 2–15), male normal emission (vv. 16–18), female normal menstrual discharge (niddah [נִדָּה], vv. 19–24), and female abnormal discharge (zavah [זָבָה], vv. 25–30) — is architecturally symmetrical: the two abnormal categories require identical eight-day resolution sequences with priestly mediation, while the two normal categories resolve by bathing and laundering until evening (or by seven days in the case of the niddah). The chapter treats male and female bodily life with structural equality.

The theological center is v. 31: "so that they do not die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle that is in their midst." The purity legislation is not about hygiene, shame, or moral guilt attached to bodily functions; it is about the management of the interface between holy divine Presence (in the Tabernacle, which is "in their midst") and the powerful life-forces of the body — seed and blood — which, when uncontained, transmit an impurity requiring ritual resolution before approach to the sanctuary. The regulations are all finite, accessible, and designed for restoration.

**Key term rulings for this chapter:** zav = preserved with bracketed gloss [one with a discharge] at first occurrence | zov = "discharge" throughout | shkhvat-zera = "emission of seed" | niddah = preserved with gloss [menstrual impurity] at first occurrence | zavah = referenced as "the one with a discharge" in text (participial form follows MT) | tameh/tahor = "unclean"/"clean" | chattat = "sin offering" | olah = "burnt offering" | torah = "instruction" | bney yisrael = "sons of Israel" | YHWH = "the LORD" | mishkan = "tabernacle" | ohel moed = "tent of meeting" | mayim chayyim = "fresh water" | ha-davah = "the one who is sick in her menstrual impurity"

---

## Consensus Translation

**1** And the LORD spoke to Moses and to Aaron, saying,

**2** "Speak to the sons of Israel and say to them: Any man who has a discharge [zav — זָב, one with an abnormal genital discharge] from his body — his discharge is unclean.

**3** And this shall be his uncleanness in his discharge: whether his body flows with his discharge or his body retains his discharge — it is his uncleanness.

**4** Every bed on which the one with the discharge lies shall be unclean, and every implement on which he sits shall be unclean.

**5** And anyone who touches his bed shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**6** And whoever sits on anything on which the one with the discharge has sat shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**7** And whoever touches the body of the one with the discharge shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**8** And if the one with the discharge spits on one who is clean, that person shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**9** And every saddle on which the one with the discharge rides shall be unclean.

**10** And whoever touches anything that was under him shall be unclean until the evening. And whoever carries any of those things shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**11** And anyone whom the one with the discharge touches without having rinsed his hands in water shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**12** And an earthen vessel that the one with the discharge touches shall be broken, and every wooden vessel shall be rinsed with water.

**13** "And when the one with the discharge is cleansed of his discharge, he shall count for himself seven days for his cleansing, and he shall wash his clothes and bathe his body in fresh water, and he shall be clean.

**14** And on the eighth day he shall take for himself two turtledoves or two young pigeons and come before the LORD to the entrance of the tent of meeting and give them to the priest.

**15** And the priest shall offer them — one as a sin offering and one as a burnt offering. And the priest shall make atonement for him before the LORD for his discharge.

**16** "If a man has an emission of seed, he shall bathe his entire body in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**17** And every garment and every skin on which there is an emission of seed shall be washed in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**18** And if a man lies with a woman and there is an emission of seed, both of them shall bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**19** "And when a woman has a discharge, and her discharge in her body is blood, she shall be in her menstrual impurity [niddah — נִדָּה] for seven days. And everyone who touches her shall be unclean until the evening.

**20** And everything on which she lies during her menstrual impurity shall be unclean, and everything on which she sits shall be unclean.

**21** And whoever touches her bed shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**22** And whoever touches anything on which she has sat shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**23** Whether it is the bed or anything on which she sits — when he touches it he shall be unclean until the evening.

**24** And if a man does indeed lie with her and her menstrual impurity comes upon him, he shall be unclean seven days, and every bed on which he lies shall be unclean.

**25** "And if a woman has a discharge of blood for many days, not at the time of her menstrual impurity, or if she has a discharge beyond her menstrual impurity, all the days of her unclean discharge shall be as the days of her menstrual impurity: she is unclean.

**26** Every bed on which she lies all the days of her discharge shall be to her as the bed of her menstrual impurity, and everything on which she sits shall be unclean, as in the uncleanness of her menstrual impurity.

**27** And whoever touches these things shall be unclean, and shall wash his clothes and bathe in water and shall be unclean until the evening.

**28** But if she is cleansed from her discharge, she shall count for herself seven days, and afterward she shall be clean.

**29** And on the eighth day she shall take for herself two turtledoves or two young pigeons and bring them to the priest at the entrance of the tent of meeting.

**30** And the priest shall offer one as a sin offering and one as a burnt offering. And the priest shall make atonement for her before the LORD for her unclean discharge.

**31** "Thus you shall separate the sons of Israel from their uncleanness, lest they die in their uncleanness by defiling my tabernacle that is in their midst.

**32** This is the instruction for the one with a discharge and for the one from whom there goes out an emission of seed, becoming unclean by it,

**33** and for the one who is sick in her menstrual impurity [niddah], and for anyone — male or female — who has a discharge, and for the man who lies with a woman who is unclean."

---

## Verse-by-Verse Ruling Notes

**V. 1 — Dual address.** All three agents: "the LORD spoke to Moses and to Aaron." EMET alone uses "Moshe and Aharon." Consensus: "Moses and Aaron" — English name forms throughout, per project convention.

**V. 2 — ish ish / "any man."** EMET: "any man" (doubled indefinite idiom). LOGOS: "any man." PNEUMA: "any man." All three agree on the idiom's rendering. The doubled ish ish is a Hebrew generalizing idiom; all agents correctly render it as "any man."

**V. 2 — zav gloss.** EMET: preserves zav with full notes in-text; the gloss "one with a discharge" is embedded in translation. LOGOS: does not transliterate zav in the verse text; uses "the one who has the discharge" throughout as the nominal equivalent. PNEUMA: "the man with the discharge" — no transliteration. Consensus: "discharge [zav — זָב, one with an abnormal genital discharge]" on first occurrence only, following the project convention of bracketed glosses for technical Hebrew terms (cf. niddah, ketonet passim). Thereafter "the one with the discharge" follows LOGOS's clean formal rendering.

**V. 2 — mibsaro / "from his body."** EMET: "from his flesh." LOGOS: "from his body." PNEUMA: "from his body." Consensus: "from his body" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; "body" is the natural English for the priestly euphemism besaro, which refers to the genital region. EMET's "flesh" is not wrong (basar = flesh) but "body" is the established rendering in this context (cf. LEV_012 and LEV_013 series).

**V. 3 — rur vs. hechtim / "flows" vs. "retains."** EMET: "whether his body flows ... or his body has retained his discharge." LOGOS: "whether his body flows with his discharge or his body is stopped from his discharge." PNEUMA: "whether his body lets the discharge flow, or his body stops up the discharge." The Hebrew presents two states of the condition: rur (active flow) and hechtim (from chatam, "to seal" — the condition where the discharge is retained or blocked). LOGOS's "stopped" and PNEUMA's "stops up" both read hechtim as cessation, following the LXX (ἐπιλέλοιπεν, "has ceased"). EMET's "retained" follows the Hebrew root chatam more precisely: the discharge is sealed/retained internally, not merely stopped. Consensus: "retains" — the MT's hechtim (from the root meaning "to seal, to stop up, to block") implies retention of what is already present rather than simple cessation. Both the active flow and the retained/blocked discharge render the man unclean. See Disagreement Log, #3.

**V. 4 — ha-keli / "implement" vs. "vessel" vs. "thing."** EMET: "implement." LOGOS: "everything on which he sits." PNEUMA: "everything he sits on." Consensus: "every implement on which he sits" — the Hebrew keli ("vessel, implement, object") is more specific than the generic "everything" of LOGOS and PNEUMA, though their rendering captures the functional meaning. "Implement" preserves the technical vocabulary of the chapter, which carefully distinguishes mishkav (lying-surface), keli yoshev (sitting-object), and merkav (riding-implement).

**V. 8 — spit transmission.** EMET: "if the zav spits upon a clean person." LOGOS: "if the one who has the discharge spits on one who is clean." PNEUMA: "if the man with the discharge spits on someone who is clean." Consensus: follows LOGOS closely; the formulation "one who is clean" is cleaner English than PNEUMA's "someone who is clean" in a legal context.

**V. 9 — merkav / "saddle."** EMET: "riding-implement." LOGOS: "saddle." PNEUMA: "saddle." Consensus: "saddle" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree. The Hebrew merkav (from rakhav, "to ride") denotes a riding-implement broadly, but "saddle" is the conventional English rendering and is the most accessible for readers. The LXX uses ἐπίσαγμα (episagma, "pack-saddle"), which is roughly equivalent.

**V. 11 — lo shafal be-mayim / "without having rinsed his hands."** EMET: "without rinsing his hands in water." LOGOS: "without having rinsed his hands in water." PNEUMA: "without having rinsed his hands in water." Consensus: "without having rinsed his hands in water" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; the perfect participle "having rinsed" captures the precondition sense of the Hebrew clause better than EMET's simple present.

**V. 12 — yishaver / "broken" vs. "smashed."** EMET: "shall be broken." LOGOS: "shall be broken." PNEUMA: "must be smashed." Consensus: "shall be broken" — EMET and LOGOS agree. The niphal of shavar = "shall be broken" (passive); "smashed" (PNEUMA) is somewhat more emphatic than the Hebrew requires, though both are defensible.

**V. 13 — mayim chayyim / "fresh water."** EMET: "living water." LOGOS: "fresh water." PNEUMA: "fresh water." Consensus: "fresh water" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree; "fresh water" is the established rendering for mayim chayyim in this project (cf. LEV_014 ruling). EMET's "living water" is the more literal translation of chayyim ("living, alive") but "fresh water" renders the functional meaning — running or spring water required for the cleansing ritual — and is the established consensus term.

**V. 13 — "cleansed" vs. "healed."** EMET: "when the zav is cleansed." LOGOS: "when the one who has the discharge is cleansed." PNEUMA: "when the man with the discharge is healed." Consensus: "cleansed" — EMET and LOGOS agree. The Hebrew yihar is from tahar ("to be/become clean, to be cleansed") — a ritual-purity term, not a healing term. PNEUMA's "healed" imports a medical register not in the Hebrew. The chapter addresses ritual status, not medical recovery; "cleansed" is the correct term.

**V. 14 — "two young pigeons" vs. "two pigeons."** EMET: "two young doves." LOGOS: "two pigeons." PNEUMA: "two young pigeons." Consensus: "two young pigeons" — the Hebrew shnei benei yonah = "two sons of the dove" = two young pigeons. PNEUMA preserves "young." LOGOS drops it. EMET's "doves" for yonah is not standard in this project. "Two young pigeons" follows PNEUMA and is consistent with LEV_012 v. 8 ruling.

**V. 15 — chattat order.** All three agents: the chattat (sin offering) precedes the olah (burnt offering) in the verse text. This is the fixed cultic order throughout Leviticus (cf. chs. 4–5, 12, 14). All agents agree. Consensus retains the chattat-then-olah sequence.

**V. 16 — kol-besaro / "entire body."** EMET: "his entire flesh." LOGOS: "his whole body." PNEUMA: "his whole body." Consensus: "his entire body" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree on "body"; "entire" from EMET's kol ("all, every") is preferable to "whole" for English cadence in formal legal prose.

**V. 17 — or / "skin" vs. "leather."** EMET: "every skin." LOGOS: "every skin." PNEUMA: "every leather." Consensus: "every skin" — the Hebrew or means "skin, hide, leather." EMET and LOGOS both use "skin," which preserves the range of meaning (animal hide used for garments or vessels). PNEUMA's "leather" is narrower.

**V. 18 — shneihem / "both of them."** EMET: "they shall both bathe." LOGOS: "both of them shall bathe." PNEUMA: "both of them shall bathe." Consensus: "both of them shall bathe" — LOGOS and PNEUMA agree. The Hebrew shneihem ("both of them / the two of them") is important: the obligation applies to both partners equally. "Both of them shall bathe" makes this explicit.

**V. 19 — opening clause structure.** EMET: "And a woman — when her discharge is blood in her flesh." LOGOS: "And if a woman has a discharge, and her discharge in her body is blood." PNEUMA: "When a woman has her regular discharge — her monthly period." Consensus: follows LOGOS's structure closely. PNEUMA's addition "her regular discharge — her monthly period" as an expansion is not in the Hebrew and smooths over the legal precision of the MT. The MT says simply: "when a woman has a discharge and her discharge in her body is blood." PNEUMA's gloss "regular" and "monthly period" are interpretive additions. See Disagreement Log, #14.

**V. 19 — niddah gloss.** EMET: "in her niddah [נִדָּה]." LOGOS: "menstrual impurity [niddah]." PNEUMA: no niddah transliteration — uses "uncleanness of her flow." Consensus: "menstrual impurity [niddah — נִדָּה]" — preserves the technical term with gloss, following project convention and LEV_012 precedent (cf. niddah rulings there). "Menstrual impurity" renders the root sense of separation/exclusion. PNEUMA's omission of the term is a loss; niddah recurs throughout chs. 15, 18, 20 and must be flagged on first occurrence.

**V. 24 — shakhov yishkav / emphatic infinitive.** EMET: "if a man does indeed lie with her" (correctly captures the doubled infinitive absolute). LOGOS: "if any man lies with her." PNEUMA: "if a man actually lies with her." Consensus: "if a man does indeed lie with her" — the MT shakhov yishkav uses the cognate infinitive absolute for emphasis, indicating a deliberate act. EMET's rendering captures this emphasis; LOGOS drops it; PNEUMA's "actually" captures it informally. "Does indeed" is more suitable to the legal register.

**V. 24 — niddat'ah alav / "her menstrual impurity comes upon him."** EMET: "her niddah is upon him." LOGOS: "her menstrual impurity comes upon him." PNEUMA: "her menstrual flow touches him." Consensus: "her menstrual impurity comes upon him" — LOGOS's rendering is the clearest and most faithful: niddat'ah (her niddah) + alav (upon him) + tehi (comes to be). "Comes upon him" renders the dynamic of transmission. EMET's "is upon him" is more static; PNEUMA's "touches him" softens the Hebrew idiom.

**V. 25 — dam rav / "many days" and structure.** EMET: "blood ... many days." LOGOS: "discharge of blood for many days." PNEUMA: "discharge of blood lasting many days." Consensus: "discharge of blood for many days" — follows LOGOS. The Hebrew yamim rabbim ("many days") modifies the duration of the blood-discharge. LOGOS's "for many days" is the standard formal equivalent.

**V. 25 — be-lo' et niddat'ah / "not at the time of her menstrual impurity."** EMET: "not at the time of her niddah." LOGOS: "not at the time of her menstrual impurity." PNEUMA: "outside the time of her monthly period." Consensus: "not at the time of her menstrual impurity" — LOGOS is precise. PNEUMA's "monthly period" again substitutes casual language for the legal term niddah. "Menstrual impurity" is the established gloss.

**V. 25 — al niddat'ah / "beyond her menstrual impurity."** EMET: "beyond her niddah." LOGOS: "beyond the time of her menstrual impurity." PNEUMA: "beyond her period." Consensus: "beyond her menstrual impurity" — LOGOS adds "the time of" which is not in the Hebrew (al niddat'ah = "upon / beyond her niddah"), but it aids clarity. Consensus follows LOGOS without "the time of" for concision: "beyond her menstrual impurity."

**V. 26 — ke-mishkav niddat'ah / "as the bed of her menstrual impurity."** All three agents agree on "as the bed of her menstrual impurity" (LOGOS) or similar. Consensus: "as the bed of her menstrual impurity" — LOGOS and EMET agree. PNEUMA's "period bed" is too casual for the legal register.

**V. 28 — ve-achar tithe'har / "afterward she shall be clean."** EMET: "and afterward she shall be clean." LOGOS: "after that she shall be clean." PNEUMA: "after that she will be clean." Consensus: "afterward she shall be clean" — EMET's "afterward" for ve-achar is correct and more formal than LOGOS's "after that."

**V. 31 — ve-hizartem / "you shall separate."** EMET: "and you shall separate." LOGOS: "you shall separate." PNEUMA: "you shall thus keep the people of Israel separate." Consensus: "Thus you shall separate" — the adverb "thus" (from the standard summary-formula beginning) is implied in the verse's position as the culminating instruction. LOGOS follows the MT closely. PNEUMA substitutes "people of Israel" for bney Yisra'el; the project ruling is "sons of Israel."

**V. 31 — mishkani / "my tabernacle."** All three agents: "my tabernacle." Consensus: "my tabernacle" — unanimous. The first-person possessive mishkani is theologically significant and is preserved by all three.

**V. 32 — "instruction" for ha-torah.** EMET: "the Torah." LOGOS: "the law." PNEUMA: "the instruction." Consensus: "instruction" — the established project ruling for torah in summary-formula contexts (cf. LEV_012, v. 7 ruling, confirmed throughout ch. 11–14 colophons).

**V. 32 — structure of summary.** All three agents provide the same summary list structure; the main divergence is in the rendering of le-tame'ah-bo ("becoming unclean by it"). EMET: "to become unclean by it." LOGOS: "becoming unclean by it." PNEUMA: "becomes unclean from it." Consensus: "becoming unclean by it" — LOGOS's participial rendering is the most fluent in English.

**V. 33 — ha-davah be-niddat'ah / "the one who is sick in her menstrual impurity."** EMET: "the one ailing in her niddah." LOGOS: "her who is sick in her menstrual impurity." PNEUMA: "the woman in the uncleanness of her monthly period." Consensus: "the one who is sick in her menstrual impurity [niddah]" — the Hebrew ha-davah (from davah, "to be sick, faint, weak") is not simply "menstruating woman" but "the ailing/faint one" — emphasizing the physical dimension of the condition. The LXX renders this as ἀδιάθετος ("the indisposed one"). LOGOS's "sick in her menstrual impurity" captures both elements. PNEUMA's rendering loses ha-davah entirely. The bracketed [niddah] is added here because the full niddah formula appears; this is the final occurrence of the term in the chapter.

**V. 33 — la-zakhar ve-la-neqevah / "male or female."** EMET: "of the male and of the female." LOGOS: "male or female." PNEUMA: "any man or woman." Consensus: "male or female" — LOGOS is precise; the Hebrew uses zakhar/neqevah (the sex-terms, not social roles). PNEUMA's "man or woman" is close but loses the register of the legal sex-terms.

---

## Textual Notes for the Consensus Record

**On the LXX rendering of zav (vv. 2–15, 32–33):** The Vaticanus LXX renders ha-zav ("the one with the discharge") throughout as ὁ γονορρυής (ho gonorrhyēs, "the one with a genital flow"), from which the modern medical term "gonorrhea" derives. This LXX choice reflects an ancient Greek-speaking community's clinical vocabulary. The Hebrew zav (from zuv, "to flow") is broader than any single modern diagnosis; it likely encompasses any abnormal, persistent genital discharge. The consensus renders it with a bracketed gloss at first occurrence and then as "the one with the discharge" throughout, following the MT's own strategy of referring to the man by his condition category.

**On v. 3 — the two states of zov:** The MT describes two conditions of the zov that both render the man unclean: (1) his body "flows" (rur) — the active state of discharge — and (2) his body has "retained" (hechtim, from chatam, "to seal") his discharge. The LXX reads hechtim as "ceased" (ἐπιλέλοιπεν), making the two states temporal stages: flowing, and then stopping. The MT's reading (retained/sealed) envisions something more like intermission with persistence: the condition is present even when not actively flowing. The consensus follows the MT.

**On v. 19 — the LXX's explicit "blood":** The LXX Vaticanus renders v. 19 with an explicit αἷμα ("blood") at the opening of the clause — "a woman who flows blood" — where the MT has only zo'vah ("she who flows/discharges") without specifying the substance. The MT names the substance (dam, "blood") in the following clause: "and her discharge in her body is blood." The LXX anticipates this naming. The consensus follows the MT's sequence.

**On v. 24 and vv. 31–33 — the purity theology of the chapter:** Verse 31 is the theological keystone of the entire purity section (chs. 11–15). Three elements bear emphasis: (1) Separation is from impurities, not from persons permanently — the same person who is unclean today can be clean by evening or on the eighth day; the system is designed for reintegration. (2) The consequence of neglect is death (ve-lo' yamutu, "lest they die") — not because the impurity itself is moral evil, but because accumulated, unresolved impurity defiles the Tabernacle, and the divine Presence inhabiting a defiled sanctuary is fatal to mortals. (3) The Tabernacle is "in their midst" (be-tokham) — not remote, but embedded in the camp. This proximity is the gift and the danger; the purity regulations manage the interface between them.

**On the woman with the hemorrhage (Mark 5:25–34):** The "flow of blood for twelve years" in Mark 5:25 is the precise condition of the zavah (vv. 25–30). In the Leviticus framework, twelve years of abnormal discharge meant twelve years of uncleanness and twelve years of contaminating every person and object she touched. The LXX of v. 25 uses ῥύσει αἵματος ("a flow of blood"), the identical phrase appearing in Mark 5:25 (ῥύσει αἵματος). Jesus's healing declaration in Mark 5:34 functions as a priestly declaratory pronouncement of cleanness — the structure of Leviticus 15's restoration compressed into a word.

**On the eighth day (vv. 14, 29):** The eighth-day offering for both the zav and the zavah (identical in structure) echoes the eighth-day pattern of ch. 14 (the tzara'at cleansing). Seven days of counted clean time — a complete week — followed by the day of re-entry into full covenant standing. The two birds, accessible and inexpensive, are the only offering required. Priestly mediation (kipper, "make atonement") closes the gap between the impurity-period and full access to the sanctuary. The cost is low; the restoration is complete.

---

*SOPHIA consensus completed. Source arbiter rulings logged in `disagreements/LEV_015.md`.*
